q: Understand the pathways of blood within the body, and their relationship with the anatomy of the heart and oxygenation/deoxygenation.
a: The cardiovascular system is a closed loop. Blood leaves the left heart via the aorta/arteries (oxygenated), travels to arterioles and capillaries (site of exchange), and returns via venules and veins (deoxygenated) to the right heart, then to the lungs for oxygenation, and back to the left heart.

q: What are the five major types of blood vessels?
a: 1. Arteries, 2. Arterioles, 3. Capillaries, 4. Venules, 5. Veins.

q: Compare and contrast the structure, mechanical properties, and functions of Arteries.
a: Stiff and springy walls; thick smooth muscle layer and large amounts of elastic/fibrous connective tissue. Function as a pressure reservoir, maintaining driving pressure for blood flow during ventricular relaxation via elastic recoil.

q: Compare and contrast the structure, mechanical properties, and functions of Arterioles.
a: Walls contain several layers of smooth muscle, minimal elastic/fibrous tissue. Function as the site of variable resistance, directing distribution of blood flow to tissues by selectively constricting and dilating.

q: Compare and contrast the structure, mechanical properties, and functions of Capillaries.
a: Smallest vessels; walls are a flat layer of endothelium one cell thick supported on a basal lamina, lacking smooth muscle and elastic/fibrous tissue. Function as the primary site of exchange between blood and interstitial fluid.

q: Compare and contrast the structure, mechanical properties, and functions of Venules.
a: Distinguished by convergent flow. Smallest have a thin exchange epithelium and little connective tissue (similar to capillaries); larger venules begin to have smooth muscle. Blood flows from capillaries into venules.

q: Compare and contrast the structure, mechanical properties, and functions of Veins.
a: Thinner walls than arteries with less elastic tissue, expanding easily. Contain one-way valves. Act as a volume reservoir, holding more than half of the circulatory systems blood.

q: Define Keyword: Vascular smooth muscle
a: The smooth muscle of blood vessels, which maintains a state of partial contraction at all times (muscle tone) and whose contraction depends on Ca2+ entry from extracellular fluid.

q: Define Keyword: Metarterioles (Metaarterioles)
a: Vessels branching from arterioles that are only partially surrounded by smooth muscle; they can act as bypass channels for blood to bypass capillaries and go directly to venous circulation if precapillary sphincters are closed.

q: Define Keyword: Precapillary sphincters
a: Muscle rings at metarteriole-capillary junctions that can open or close off capillaries to restrict or increase blood flow into capillary beds.

q: Define Keyword: Pericytes
a: Highly branched contractile cells that surround capillaries, forming a meshlike outer layer. They contribute to capillary permeability tightness and secrete factors influencing capillary growth.

q: Define Keyword: Volume reservoir
a: The function of the veins; they hold more than half the blood in the circulatory system and can distribute blood to the arterial side if blood pressure falls too low.

q: Discuss the role of smooth muscle in blood vessels.
a: Vascular smooth muscle maintains a state of partial contraction (muscle tone) at all times. It can contract (vasoconstriction) or relax (vasodilation) under the influence of signal molecules (neurotransmitters, hormones, paracrine signals) to regulate vessel diameter and resistance.

q: Define Keyword: Vasoconstriction
a: Narrowing of the diameter of the blood vessel lumen.

q: Define Keyword: Vasodilation
a: Widening of the diameter of the blood vessel lumen.

q: Define Keyword: Muscle tone
a: The state of partial contraction maintained at all times by smooth muscle in most blood vessels.

q: Explain what creates blood pressure.
a: Ventricular contraction is the force that creates blood flow. Pressure produced by the left ventricle is stored in the elastic walls of arteries and slowly released through elastic recoil, maintaining continuous driving pressure.

q: Explain how blood pressure changes as blood flows through the systemic circulation.
a: Blood pressure is highest in the arteries and decreases continuously as blood flows through the circulatory system, due to energy lost from resistance to flow and friction. It is lowest in the veins and venae cavae.

q: Define Keyword: Systolic pressure
a: The higher value of blood pressure during ventricular systole (contraction). The typical average high in the aorta is 120 mm Hg.

q: Define Keyword: Diastolic pressure
a: The lower value of blood pressure during ventricular diastole (relaxation). The typical low in the aorta is 80 mm Hg.

q: Define Keyword: Pulse (pressure wave)
a: The rapid pressure increase when the left ventricle pushes blood into the aorta, which can be felt in the arteries and travels about 10 times faster than the blood itself.

q: Define Keyword: Pulse pressure
a: A measure of the strength of the pressure wave. Formula: Systolic pressure minus diastolic pressure.

q: Define Keyword: Respiratory pump
a: A mechanism associated with breathing that aids the return of venous blood to the heart.

q: Define Keyword: Skeletal [muscle] pump
a: A mechanism where skeletal muscle contractions compress veins, forcing blood upward past one-way valves to aid venous return to the heart.

q: Explain the relationship between blood flow, pressure gradients, and the resistance of the system to flow.
a: Blood flow is directly proportional to the pressure gradient between any two points and inversely proportional to the resistance of the vessels to flow. (Flow is proportional to Delta P / R).

q: Describe how blood pressure is estimated using sphygmomanometry.
a: A sphygmomanometer cuff is inflated around the upper arm above systolic pressure to stop arterial flow. As pressure is released, the pressure at which Korotkoff sounds (thumping) are first heard is systolic pressure. The point where sounds disappear is diastolic pressure.

q: Define Keyword: Mean arterial pressure (MAP)
a: The driving pressure for blood flow. It is closer to diastolic pressure because diastole lasts twice as long as systole. Formula: MAP = diastolic pressure + 1/3 (systolic pressure minus diastolic pressure).

q: Define Keyword: Korotkoff sound
a: Thumping noises heard through a stethoscope during sphygmomanometry; caused by the turbulent pulsatile flow of blood squeezing through a compressed artery.

q: Explain the contributions of cardiac output and peripheral resistance to blood pressure.
a: Mean arterial pressure is a balance between blood flow into the arteries (cardiac output) and blood flow out (influenced by peripheral resistance). MAP is proportional to Cardiac Output times Resistance of arterioles.

q: Explain the multiple factors that affect blood pressure (MAP).
a: 1. Blood volume (fluid intake/loss), 2. Effectiveness of the heart as a pump (cardiac output = heart rate x stroke volume), 3. Resistance of the system to blood flow (diameter of arterioles), 4. Relative distribution of blood between arterial and venous vessels (diameter of veins).

q: List the three mechanisms controlling arteriolar resistance.
a: 1. Local control of arteriolar resistance (matches tissue blood flow to metabolic needs), 2. Sympathetic reflexes mediated by CNS (maintain MAP and distribution), 3. Hormones (influence BP by acting directly on arterioles and altering autonomic reflex control).

q: Describe the hormonal and neural control of blood vessel diameter, including significant neurotransmitters and their receptor types.
a: Sympathetic neurons release norepinephrine (binds alpha-receptors for vasoconstriction). Epinephrine from adrenal medulla binds alpha-receptors (vasoconstriction) and beta-2-receptors in heart/liver/skeletal muscle (vasodilation).

q: Define Keyword: Myogenic autoregulation
a: Vascular smooth muscles ability to regulate its own state of contraction. When an increase in BP stretches the muscle, mechanically gated channels open, leading to depolarization and calcium entry, causing the arteriole to automatically constrict.

q: Define Keyword: Active hyperemia
a: The process in which an increase in blood flow accompanies an increase in metabolic activity. Decreased O2 and increased CO2 dilate arterioles to bring in additional O2 and remove waste.

q: Define Keyword: Reactive hyperemia
a: An increase in tissue blood flow following a period of low perfusion (decreased blood flow due to occlusion). Accumulated vasodilators like NO and CO2 immediately trigger significant vasodilation once flow resumes.

q: Explain the multiple factors that affect blood flow.
a: Flow is proportional to the pressure gradient (MAP minus right atrial pressure) and inversely proportional to resistance. Resistance increases with vessel length and fluid viscosity, and is inversely proportional to the fourth power of the vessel radius.

q: Describe in detail the steps of the baroreceptor reflex (Stimulus, Sensor, Afferent, Integrating Center).
a: Stimulus: Increase in blood pressure. Sensor: Baroreceptors in carotid arteries and aorta increase firing rate. Afferent: Sensory neurons. Integrating center: Cardiovascular control center (CVCC) in the medulla oblongata.

q: Describe in detail the steps of the baroreceptor reflex (Output, Target, Response).
a: Output: Decreased sympathetic output and increased parasympathetic output. Targets: Arteriolar smooth muscle dilates, ventricular myocardium decreases force of contraction, SA node decreases heart rate. Systemic response: Decrease in blood pressure.

q: Define Keyword: Cardiovascular control center (CVCC)
a: A CNS network in the medulla oblongata that acts as the main integrating center for cardiovascular reflex control, ensuring adequate blood flow to the brain and heart by maintaining sufficient MAP.

q: Define Keyword: Baroreceptors
a: Tonically active, stretch-sensitive mechanoreceptors located in the walls of the carotid arteries and aorta that monitor arterial blood pressure.

q: Review Question 2: Match vessel types (arterioles, arteries, capillaries, veins, venules) to: 1. Store pressure generated by the heart, 2. Have walls that are both stiff and elastic.
a: 1. Arteries, 2. Arteries.

q: Review Question 2: Match vessel types to: 3. Carry low-oxygen blood, 4. Have thin walls of exchange epithelium.
a: 3. Veins and Venules (in systemic circulation), 4. Capillaries and Venules.

q: Review Question 2: Match vessel types to: 5. Act as a volume reservoir, 6. Their diameter can be altered by neural input.
a: 5. Veins, 6. Arterioles and Veins.

q: Review Question 2: Match vessel types to: 7. Blood flow slowest through these vessels, 8. Have lowest blood pressure, 9. Are the main site of variable resistance.
a: 7. Capillaries and Venules, 8. Veins (specifically venae cavae), 9. Arterioles.

q: Review Question 4: Blood flow to individual tissues is regulated by selective vasoconstriction and vasodilation of which vessels?
a: Arterioles.

q: Review Question 6: The rapid pressure increase that occurs when the left ventricle pushes blood into the aorta can be felt as a pressure wave, or [what]? What is the equation used to calculate its strength?
a: Pulse. The strength is Pulse Pressure. Equation: Systolic pressure minus diastolic pressure.

q: Review Question 9: When measuring a person's blood pressure, at what point in the procedure are you likely to hear Korotkoff sounds?
a: When cuff pressure is between the systolic and diastolic arterial blood pressures, causing turbulent, pulsatile blood flow through the compressed artery.

q: Review Question 10: List three paracrine molecules that cause vasodilation. What is the source of each one?
a: 1. Nitric oxide (NO) from endothelium; 2. Adenosine from hypoxic cells (myocardium); 3. Histamine from mast cells; (Also: low O2, high CO2, H+, K+ from cell metabolism; Bradykinin from multiple tissues).

q: Review Question 10: In addition to paracrine signals, list two other ways to control smooth muscle contraction in arterioles.
a: 1. Sympathetic reflexes mediated by the CNS (neural control); 2. Hormones (e.g., atrial natriuretic peptide, angiotensin II, epinephrine).

q: Review Question 11: What is hyperemia? How does active hyperemia differ from reactive hyperemia?
a: Hyperemia is a locally mediated increase in blood flow. Active hyperemia accompanies an increase in metabolic activity. Reactive hyperemia follows a period of decreased or occluded blood flow (low perfusion).

q: Review Question 12: Most systemic arterioles are innervated by the __________ branch of the nervous system. Increased sympathetic input will have what effect on arteriole diameter?
a: Sympathetic branch. Increased sympathetic input causes vasoconstriction (decreases arteriole diameter).

q: Review Question 13: Match the event Vasoconstriction of intestinal arterioles with appropriate neurotransmitter(s) and receptor(s).
a: Norepinephrine or Epinephrine binding to alpha-receptors.

q: Review Question 13: Match the event Vasodilation of coronary arterioles with appropriate neurotransmitter(s) and receptor(s).
a: Epinephrine binding to beta-2-receptors.

q: Review Question 13: Match the event Increased heart rate with appropriate neurotransmitter(s) and receptor(s).
a: Norepinephrine or Epinephrine binding to beta-1-receptors.

q: Review Question 13: Match the event Decreased heart rate with appropriate neurotransmitter(s) and receptor(s).
a: Acetylcholine binding to Muscarinic receptors.

q: Review Question 21: Calcium channel blockers prevent Ca2+ movement through Ca2+ channels. Explain two ways this action lowers blood pressure.
a: 1. Decreases contractility (causes vasodilation) of vascular smooth muscle. 2. Decreases the depolarization rate of the SA node and decreases force of contraction in the heart.

q: Review Question 21: Why are neurons and other cells unaffected by Calcium channel blockers used for hypertension?
a: Other tissues with Ca2+ channels (e.g., neurons) are only minimally affected because their Ca2+ channels are of a different subtype than those in vascular smooth muscle and cardiac muscle.

q: Review Question 23: Map all the following factors that influence mean arterial pressure: blood volume, cardiac output, heart rate, stroke volume, peripheral resistance.
a: MAP is proportional to Cardiac Output x Peripheral Resistance. Cardiac Output is determined by Heart Rate x Stroke Volume. MAP is also influenced by Blood Volume.

q: Review Question 24: Define myogenic autoregulation. What mechanisms have been proposed to explain it?
a: It is vascular smooth muscles ability to regulate its own state of contraction. Mechanism: Stretch opens mechanically gated channels, causing cation entry, depolarization, opening of voltage-gated Ca2+ channels, Ca2+ entry, and contraction.

q: Review Question 27a: If resistance in vessels 1 and 2 increases because of local paracrine signals but cardiac output is unchanged, what happens to MAP? What happens to flow through vessels 1 and 2? Through vessels 3 and 4?
a: MAP increases (because total peripheral resistance increases). Flow through vessels 1 and 2 decreases. Flow is diverted and distributed to lower-resistance vessels, so flow through vessels 3 and 4 increases.

q: Review Question 28: Based on frog heart contractions with Epinephrine, ACh, and Atropine, what conclusion can you draw about the mechanism of action of atropine?
a: Atropine blocks the parasympathetic effects of Acetylcholine on heart rate (preventing the decrease in heart rate), but does not block sympathetic effects of Epinephrine. It is likely a muscarinic receptor antagonist.

q: Review Question 35: Calculate the mean arterial pressure (MAP) and pulse pressure for a person with a blood pressure of 115/73.
a: Pulse pressure = 115 - 73 = 42 mm Hg. MAP = 73 + 1/3 (42) = 73 + 14 = 87 mm Hg.

q: Concept Check 2: If you check the pulse in a person's carotid artery and left wrist at the same time, would the pressure waves occur simultaneously? Explain.
a: No. The pressure wave travels outward from the heart. The carotid artery is closer to the heart than the left wrist, so the pulse at the carotid artery would be felt slightly earlier.

q: Concept Check 3: Who has the higher pulse pressure, someone with blood pressure of 90/60 or someone with blood pressure of 130/95?
a: The person with 130/95. Pulse pressure = systolic - diastolic. 90-60 = 30 mm Hg. 130-95 = 35 mm Hg.

q: Concept Check 4: If heart rate increases, would the contribution of systolic pressure to mean arterial pressure decrease or increase, and would MAP decrease or increase?
a: Since diastole normally lasts twice as long as systole, an increased HR shortens diastole. The contribution of systolic pressure to MAP would increase, and MAP would increase.

q: Concept Check 5: Peter's systolic pressure is 112 mm Hg, and his diastolic pressure is 68 mm Hg. What is his pulse pressure? His MAP?
Concept Check 6: Resistance to blood flow is determined primarily by which? (a) blood viscosity, (b) blood volume, (c) cardiac output, (d) blood vessel diameter, or (e) blood pressure gradient.
a: (d) blood vessel diameter (specifically, the radius of the arterioles).

q: Concept Check 7: The extracellular fluid concentration of K+ increases in exercising skeletal muscles. What effect does this increase in K+ have on blood flow in the muscles?
a: Increased K+ is a metabolic paracrine signal (indicative of increased cellular activity) that causes vasodilation, thereby increasing blood flow to the exercising muscles.

q: Concept Check 8: What happens when epinephrine combines with beta-1-receptors in the heart? With beta-2-receptors in the heart?
a: Beta-1 receptors on the SA node and ventricular myocardium increase heart rate and force of contraction. Beta-2 receptors on coronary arterioles cause vasodilation, increasing blood flow to heart muscle.

q: Concept Check 9: Skeletal muscle arterioles have both alpha- and beta-receptors on their smooth muscle. Epinephrine can bind to both. Will these arterioles constrict or dilate in response to epinephrine? Explain.
a: They will dilate. Although epinephrine can bind to both, it has a higher affinity for beta-2 receptors (which cause vasodilation) than for alpha-receptors in these specific tissues.

q: Concept Check 12: Map the reflex response to orthostatic hypotension.
a: Stimulus: Drop in BP upon standing. Sensor: Baroreceptors decrease firing rate. Afferent: Sensory neurons. Integrating Center: CVCC. Output: Increased sympathetic, decreased parasympathetic. Target/Response: Arterioles/Veins constrict, SA node increases HR, Ventricles increase force. Systemic: BP increases to normal.

q: Concept Check 13: Map a reflex map showing the cardiovascular response to a fight-or-flight situation.
a: Stimulus: Fear. Integrating Center: CVCC & hypothalamus. Output: Increased sympathetic & adrenal epinephrine. Responses: SA node increases HR, myocardium increases force, systemic arterioles constrict (alpha), skeletal/heart arterioles dilate (beta-2). Cardiac output increases.

q: END
a: END
